text.title: Overlay — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for overlay directory layout, precedence resolution, and audit.

rule.statement: Overlays live at EXACTLY two locations, no others: (a) USER `~/.faion/overrides/<tier>/<group>/<slug>/` (b) REPO `<repo>/.faion/overrides/<tier>/<group>/<slug>/`. Each overlay mirrors the methodology directory structure (`AGENTS.md` + `content/*.xml` + optional templates/scripts).

rationale.source: Helm values + git config FILES precedence

rule.rationale: Two-level precedence (user, project) covers ~95% of real cases (npm, pip, git, Docker all use this pattern). Adding more levels (machine-wide, group-wide) is rarely needed and adds discovery cost.

rule.statement: Precedence is FIXED: REPO > USER > SHIPPED. For a methodology path P, the resolver checks `<repo>/.faion/overrides/P/` first, then `~/.faion/overrides/P/`, then the shipped `faion-network/skills/faion/knowledge/P/`. The first match wins per-file (not per-directory).
rule.rationale: Per-file resolution lets a team override AGENTS.md but inherit content/*.xml from upstream. Per-directory resolution would force teams to copy entire methodologies for one-line changes.

rule.statement: Three explicit merge modes declared in `templates/overrides-config.yaml`: REPLACE (default — overlay file replaces shipped file), PREPEND (overlay content inserted before shipped), APPEND (overlay content inserted after shipped). Modes apply per-file. PREPEND/APPEND only work on `content/*.xml` files with the same `<text id>`.
rule.rationale: Most overrides want REPLACE; some (e.g. company-specific failure-modes) want APPEND. Explicit declaration prevents silent merge surprises.

rule.statement: The validator MUST detect and surface 4 conflict types: (a) overlay file has different `content_id` than shipped (version skew), (b) overlay declares APPEND but shipped file's schema doesn't allow extensions, (c) overlay overrides a methodology marked `non-overridable: true` in shipped frontmatter, (d) overlay introduces a frontmatter key not in REQUIRED_FRONTMATTER_KEYS.

rationale.source: Helm template validation + JSON Schema gates

rule.rationale: Silent overlay errors cause hard-to-debug behaviour in production. Pre-runtime validation is the only sustainable boundary.

rule.statement: Every resolved methodology view MUST emit an audit event listing: methodology path, source (REPO/USER/SHIPPED), merge mode, overlay maintainer, resolved_at timestamp. Audit log persists to `.faion/audit/overlay-log.ndjson`.
rule.rationale: Compliance audits ask "show me what content actually ran for engagement X". Without an audit log, the team cannot prove the answer. The ndjson log is cheap and machine-grepable.

example.title: Resolved view for ADR template
example.code:
Methodology path: pro/dev/software-developer/adr-architecture-decisions/
Step 1: check repo/.faion/overrides/pro/dev/.../adr.../ found: templates/adr-template.md → use repo's not found: AGENTS.md → continue not found: content/*.xml → continue
Step 2: check ~/.faion/overrides/pro/dev/.../adr.../ found: AGENTS.md → use user's not found: content/*.xml → continue
Step 3: shipped found: content/*.xml → use shipped
Audit event: { path: "pro/dev/.../adr-architecture-decisions", sources: { "templates/adr-template.md": "REPO", "AGENTS.md": "USER", "content/01-core-rules.xml": "SHIPPED", "content/02-output-contract.xml": "SHIPPED", "content/03-failure-modes.xml": "SHIPPED" }, merge_modes: { "templates/adr-template.md": "REPLACE" }, resolved_at: "2026-05-20T14:23:00Z" }
example.note: r2 + r5: per-file resolution with full audit. Team's template wins; methodology rules stay shipped.

example.title: Directory-level override (r2 violation)
example.code:
Team copies entire `pro/dev/software-developer/adr-architecture-decisions/` into their overrides folder to change ONE line in templates/adr-template.md.
Six months later: shipped adds new failure modes (CVE-driven). Team's copy is stale; team never sees the new rules.
example.note: Violates r2 per-file principle. Directory-level forks defeat the overlay's purpose.
